limitation: This is a heuristic scanner for agent review triage, not a security, performance, accessibility, or design oracle. It can miss issues and produce false positives; verify findings manually against current WordPress docs, profiling, and real UI review.

findings.message: Plugin registers a top-level admin menu.
findings.why: Top-level menus add navigation weight and should be reserved for primary plugin workflows.
findings.remediation: Confirm the feature deserves top-level placement; otherwise prefer an appropriate submenu such as Settings, Tools, or a product-specific parent.

findings.message: Admin form has no obvious Settings API or nonce flow.
findings.why: Good UI must preserve secure save behavior and clear state-changing intent.
findings.remediation: Use Settings API with settings_fields() or add wp_nonce_field() plus a capability and nonce check in the handler.

findings.message: Admin page markup has no obvious .wrap container.
findings.why: Classic WordPress admin pages feel more native and inherit spacing when using the standard wrap structure.
findings.remediation: Render the page inside <div class="wrap plugin-root-class"> unless a custom app shell is explicitly justified.

findings.message: Admin UI file has no obvious page h1.
findings.why: A clear page heading anchors the screen for visual, keyboard, and screen-reader users.
findings.remediation: Add one escaped, translatable <h1> that describes the current admin screen.

findings.message: Admin assets are enqueued without an obvious screen check.
findings.why: Unscoped admin CSS/JS can alter unrelated WordPress screens and slow the admin experience.
findings.remediation: Gate admin assets by $hook_suffix or get_current_screen()->id.

findings.message: Action label is vague.
findings.why: Specific button text reduces mistakes and helps users predict what will happen.
findings.remediation: Use task-specific labels such as Save settings, Connect account, Regenerate cache, or Import items.

findings.message: Form control has no obvious label or accessible name nearby.
findings.why: Controls without names are difficult or impossible to use with assistive technology.
findings.remediation: Add a visible <label for="..."> or a justified aria-label/aria-labelledby association.

findings.message: Placeholder appears to be used without a real label.
findings.why: Placeholder text disappears as users type and is not a reliable accessible name.
findings.remediation: Keep the placeholder optional and add a persistent visible label.

findings.message: Required field has no obvious visible or screen-reader explanation nearby.
findings.why: Users need to know which fields are required before submission.
findings.remediation: Add text or screen-reader text that explains required fields and keep server-side validation.

findings.message: Form control has no obvious label or accessible name nearby.
findings.why: Controls without names are difficult or impossible to use with assistive technology.
findings.remediation: Add a visible <label for="..."> or a justified aria-label/aria-labelledby association.

findings.message: Checkbox/radio control has no nearby fieldset/legend.
findings.why: Grouped choices need context so users understand what the selection controls.
findings.remediation: Wrap related checkbox/radio controls in a fieldset with a concise legend.

findings.message: Error message is not obviously associated with a field or live region.
findings.why: Users should be able to find and hear validation errors near the affected control.
findings.remediation: Connect field errors with aria-describedby and use role="alert" or wp.a11y.speak() for dynamic errors when appropriate.

findings.message: Form control has no obvious label or accessible name nearby.
findings.why: Controls without names are difficult or impossible to use with assistive technology.
findings.remediation: Add a visible <label for="..."> or a justified aria-label/aria-labelledby association.

findings.message: Placeholder appears to be used without a real label.
findings.why: Placeholder text disappears as users type and is not a reliable accessible name.
findings.remediation: Keep the placeholder optional and add a persistent visible label.

findings.message: Frontend stylesheet contains a broad global selector.
findings.why: Plugin frontend CSS should not override theme typography, spacing, or controls globally.
findings.remediation: Scope selectors under the plugin wrapper or block class.

findings.message: Frontend CSS sets a font family.
findings.why: Plugin output should usually inherit the active theme typography unless branding is explicitly configured.
findings.remediation: Remove the hardcoded font-family or make it an opt-in branded setting.

findings.message: Frontend stylesheet contains a broad global selector.
findings.why: Plugin frontend CSS should not override theme typography, spacing, or controls globally.
findings.remediation: Scope selectors under the plugin wrapper or block class.

findings.message: Frontend form has no obvious labels or accessible names.
findings.why: Public forms must be understandable to keyboard and screen-reader users.
findings.remediation: Add labels, descriptions, error states, and semantic form structure.

findings.message: Block editor file contains many inspector controls (11).
findings.why: Overloaded sidebars hide the primary editing task and increase cognitive load.
findings.remediation: Move primary content controls onto the canvas, group advanced settings, and remove controls that can use block supports.

findings.message: Block edit UI has no obvious Placeholder component.
findings.why: Blocks that require setup should explain the next step before content exists.
findings.remediation: Add a concise Placeholder with a primary setup action when the block starts empty or disconnected.

findings.message: Editor UI string is not obviously wrapped in @wordpress/i18n.
findings.why: Block editor UI text should be translation-ready.
findings.remediation: Wrap strings with __(), _x(), or related @wordpress/i18n helpers and use the plugin text domain.

findings.message: Editor UI string is not obviously wrapped in @wordpress/i18n.
findings.why: Block editor UI text should be translation-ready.
findings.remediation: Wrap strings with __(), _x(), or related @wordpress/i18n helpers and use the plugin text domain.

findings.message: Block editor control has no obvious label prop.
findings.why: Editor controls need labels for usability, accessibility, and translation context.
findings.remediation: Add a concise translatable label prop.

findings.message: Block editor control has no obvious label prop.
findings.why: Editor controls need labels for usability, accessibility, and translation context.
findings.remediation: Add a concise translatable label prop.

findings.message: Block editor control has no obvious label prop.
findings.why: Editor controls need labels for usability, accessibility, and translation context.
findings.remediation: Add a concise translatable label prop.

findings.message: Block editor control has no obvious label prop.
findings.why: Editor controls need labels for usability, accessibility, and translation context.
findings.remediation: Add a concise translatable label prop.

findings.message: ToolbarButton has no obvious accessible label/title.
findings.why: Icon-only toolbar controls need accessible names.
findings.remediation: Add a translatable label/title or aria-label that names the action.

findings.message: Admin stylesheet contains a broad/global selector.
findings.why: Plugin admin CSS should not override unrelated WordPress admin screens.
findings.remediation: Scope selectors under the plugin admin root class.

findings.message: Admin stylesheet contains a broad/global selector.
findings.why: Plugin admin CSS should not override unrelated WordPress admin screens.
findings.remediation: Scope selectors under the plugin admin root class.

findings.message: CSS removes outline without an obvious replacement focus style.
findings.why: Visible focus is required for keyboard users.
findings.remediation: Add a strong :focus or :focus-visible style when removing the browser default.

findings.message: CSS uses a fixed pixel width.
findings.why: Fixed widths can break mobile admin, narrow editor sidebars, or theme layouts.
findings.remediation: Use max-width, minmax(), flex/grid, or responsive constraints where possible.

findings.message: CSS uses physical left/right properties.
findings.why: Physical direction assumptions can make RTL layouts harder to support.
findings.remediation: Prefer logical properties such as margin-inline-start, inset-inline-end, or text-align: start where practical.

findings.message: Stylesheet uses animation/transition without a prefers-reduced-motion branch.
findings.why: Some users prefer reduced motion, and admin UI should respect that preference.
findings.remediation: Add a @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) override when motion is not essential.

findings.message: CSS uses a fixed pixel width.
findings.why: Fixed widths can break mobile admin, narrow editor sidebars, or theme layouts.
findings.remediation: Use max-width, minmax(), flex/grid, or responsive constraints where possible.

findings.message: CSS uses physical left/right properties.
findings.why: Physical direction assumptions can make RTL layouts harder to support.
findings.remediation: Prefer logical properties such as margin-inline-start, inset-inline-end, or text-align: start where practical.

design.limitation: Design findings are static heuristics. They cannot fully judge visual quality, contrast, responsiveness, usability, or real WordPress admin/editor behavior; verify with manual UI review and assistive-technology checks.
